---
title: "Genome assembly"
author: "Darius HUISMAN"
date: "2026-07-08"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Introduction

Note that this workshop is part of a general project "marine-genomics", so the initial working directory for all the data and code generated below is the file "Workshop2_assembly".

## Redbean Assembly

### Setup working directory

``` bash
mkdir redbean
cd redbean
```

### Assembly steps

Run the first step of the assembly (takes a couple minutes)

``` bash
wtdbg2 -x ont -g 5m -t 4 -i amtp4.fastq -fo amtp4
wtpoa-cns -t 4 -i amtp4.ctg.lay.gz -fo amtp4.ctg.fa
```

Inspecte assembly statistics

``` bash
assembly-stats amtp4.ctg.fa
```

Run the second step of the assembly (takes a few minutes)

``` bash
cat /pvol/data/amtp4/*.fastq > amtp4_20x.fastq
wtdbg2 -x ont -g 5m -t 4 -i amtp4_20x.fastq -fo amtp4_20x
wtpoa-cns -t 4 -i amtp4_20x.ctg.lay.gz -fo amtp4_20x.ctg.fa
```

Inspecte assembly statistics
``` bash
assembly-stats amtp4.ctg.fa
```

## Flye Assembly

Try an alternative assembler called Flye

###Setup a Flye working directory

``` bash
mkdir flye
cd flye
```

### Run Flye on both datasets

``` bash
ln -s ../redbean/amtp4.fastq .
ln -s ../redbean/amtp4_20x.fastq .
```

#### Small file
use slurm script for small files (!!! use only once !!!)

``` bash
sbatch run_flye_small.sh
```

check job status
``` bash
squeue
```
look at file generated by slurm
``` bash
tail slurm-330.out
```
follow output while it is running
``` bash
tail -f slurm-330.out
```
Inspecte assembly statistics
``` bash
assembly-stats amtp4/assembly.fasta
```

#### Large fimle
use slurm script for small files (!!! use only once !!!)
```bash
sbatch run_flye_large.sh
```
look at file generated by slurm
``` bash
tail slurm-342.out
```
follow output while it is running
``` bash
tail -f slurm-342.out
```
Inspecte assembly statistics
``` bash
assembly-stats amtp4_20x/assembly.fasta
```

## Inspect assembly graphs with Bandage
